notes: Alabama operates a five-layer AI policy stack: a binding state-agency AUP (OIT, Jan 2025), a cross-branch governance body (TQAB, Feb 2026) derived from the Governor's 2024 Task Force, two 2026 legislative instruments (SB328 proposed; HB604 enacted, effective July 2026), an interim legislative study report, a district position statement (Tuscaloosa City Schools) plus curricular programs in Birmingham/Huntsville, the ACHE AI Exchange consortium track, and HEI-level faculty guidelines (UA) and institutional policy (UNA 2024/2025).

notes: Texas operates a multi-layer AI-in-education stack: HB 2060 (88R, effective 2023-09-01) created a statewide AI Advisory Council that reported to the Legislature in late 2024 on agency AI inventories, ethics, and automated decision systems; the Department of Information Resources (DIR) administers a statewide AI Awareness Training program under Texas Gov't Code 2054.5191/5193 that covers state and local government employees and requires school-district cybersecurity coordinators to complete annual training; TEA's School Board Member Training includes a required annual Cybersecurity and Artificial Intelligence topic, and TEA maintains an AI correspondence page plus a 2025 Long-Range Plan for Technology and 2025 SB 569 virtual/hybrid guidance that frame classroom technology use. District-level policy is active (Houston ISD generativeai portal, Dallas ISD AI Playbook with grade 9-12 guidelines embedded in board policy, Lake Travis ISD student code of conduct), and higher education is organized through the Texas Higher Education Coordinating Board (THECB) AI EmpowerED webinar series plus UT Austin's statewide Generative AI project covering 74 institutions and tools such as UT Sage. The posture is governance-first and agency-led, with classroom rules still delegated to districts and institutions.

teacher_guidance.summary: Texas's teacher-facing AI posture is governance-led with emerging classroom guidance. HB 2060 (2023) created a statewide AI Advisory Council; DIR's statewide AI Awareness Training applies to government employees and school-district cybersecurity coordinators; TEA requires annual Cybersecurity and Artificial Intelligence training for school-board trustees; and classroom rules are set at the district level (Dallas ISD AI Playbook, Houston ISD generative-AI portal, Lake Travis ISD code-of-conduct AI section). Higher education is coordinated via THECB's AI EmpowerED series and UT Austin's statewide Generative AI Project (UT Sage, instructor guide, 74-institution cohort). Teachers should treat AI as permitted with disclosure at 9-12 and higher-ed, verify district policy before any graded or assessment use, and never upload student PII to public AI tools.

evidence_spans.quote: This document guides Utah students, staff, and school communities on the appropriate and responsible use of artificial intelligence (AI), particularly generative AI tools, in classroom instruction, school management, and system-wide operations where permitted by local policy.

Students and staff should not copy from any source, including generative AI, without prior approval and adequate documentation. Students should not submit AI-generated work as their original work. Staff and students will be taught how to properly cite or acknowledge the use of AI where applicable.

Staff and students are prohibited from entering confidential or personally identifiable information into unauthorized AI tools, such as those without approved data privacy agreements. This extends to contracted vendors and other third parties.

We educate our staff and students about AI. Promoting AI literacy among students and staff is central to addressing the risks of AI use and teaches critical skills for students' futures. Students and staff will be given support to develop their AI literacy, which includes how to use AI, when to use it,...
Artificial Intelligence Framework for Utah P-12 Education: Guidance on the Use of AI in Our Schools

Conversely, an outright ban on AI in schools impedes the development of essential future workforce skills. Instead, a balanced and informed approach is preferred, encompassing both maximizing learning and attending to security measures.
